observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to

(a) file a request for authority to write-off dormant and uncollectible receivable accounts to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) with the required supporting documents under Item 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005; 
(b) instruct the Acting City Accountant and City Social Welfare Development Officer (CSWDO) to reconcile their records in order to effect the necessary adjustment/correction, if any; and

(c) instruct the CSWDO to

(i) revisit the Kasunduan governing the implementation of SEA program and ensure that the provisions stipulated therein are strictly observed in order to protect the interest of the government; and

(ii) revise the format of the Kasunduan to include the loan amount, payment terms, payment method, a penal provision and further information on the borrower and the co-maker.

1.10 We reiterated and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the City Accountant
(i) exert more effort in determining the nature and details of the long-
outstanding bank reconciling items to facilitate immediate adjustments in the
books; and (ii) cause the cancellation of the stale checks and set-up a liability in
the books until the replacement.

2.17 We reiterated that the City Mayor require the (a) accountable officers to
improve further their compliance on the reporting/liquidation of cash advances;
and (b) City Accountant, in collaboration with the City Legal Officer, to
intensify collection measures or institute appropriate remedy that would
require accountable officers to settle or liquidate their past due accounts; and
(c) if conditions and circumstances on writing off the accounts would be present,
direct the City Accountant and the Legal officer to initiate the procedures for
the application of the request under COA Circular 2016-005.

3.15 We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) City Treasurer to
(i) strictly enforce the remedies for the collection of delinquent RPT as expressly
provided in Section 256 of R.A. No. 7160; (ii) on a timely basis, prepare an
accurate and updated Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due (CLTTD) and
submit the same to the Accounting Office as the basis in setting-up of RPT/SET
receivables in the beginning of the year; (ii) intensify collection efforts and/or
institute the available legal remedies to improve the tax collection efficiency of
the City; (b) City Accountant and City Treasurer to reconcile their records of
RPT/SET receivables for reliable account balances in the financial statements;
(c) City Accountant use the Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due prepared
by the CTO as the basis in recording the receivables at the beginning of the year;
and (d) City Assessor to coordinate with and on a timely basis, provide the CTO
with the needed assessment reports to facilitate the preparation of Certified List
of Taxpayers by the latter within the prescribed period.

4.10 We reiterated that the LCE instruct the (a) Inventory Committee in
coordination with the CGSO, in collaboration with all the department heads
and offices, to (i) expedite the completion of the physical count of stocks of all
supplies and materials of the City on or before July 31 and January 31 of each
year; (ii) reconcile the resultant report with the accounting records; (b) CGSO
to strictly adhere to the required maintenance of complete and updated
inventory records; (c) City Accountant to (i) maintain SLCs for each
commodity/stock; and (ii) coordinate with the CGSO and Inventory Committee
to settle the variances of Inventory records.

5.14 We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) Inventory
Committee in coordination with the City General Services Office (CGSO) and
the concerned department heads and offices to (i) expedite the completion of the
physical inventory of all PPE of the City on or before January 31 of each year;
(ii) reconcile the resultant report with the accounting records; (b) CGSO to
strictly comply with the required maintenance of complete and updated
property records; (c) City Accountant to (i) coordinate with the CGSO and
Inventory Committee for the reconciliation of respective records and effect the
necessary corrections/adjustments, if warranted, in the books of accounts; and
(ii) transfer the completed projects from the Construction in Progress (CIP)
account to the appropriate asset accounts.

6.17 We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the (a) City Accountant
should (i) initiate a thorough review and exert extra efforts to trace the original
documents and recording of non-moving Due to National Government Agencies
(NGAs) account balances in order to determine the details such as the name of
the source agency for eventual reconciliation; (ii) trace the payees of the stale
checks for possible replacement ; and (b) the Heads of the Offices concerned
strictly observe the time frame of scheduled implementation of the projects to
ensure efficient delivery of public services to the intended beneficiaries and to
avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future. Furthermore, the City
Treasurer should remit to the Bureau of Treasury the unexpended balance of
the LGSF.

7.12 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) City
Treasurer to (i) discontinue the imposition upon taxpayers of unauthorized
fees and charges unless proven that the act of collection is supported by an
ordinance enacted by the Sanggunian Panlungsod; and (ii) responsible
Department Heads to submit justification on the disputed charges against the
set-aside trust fund and henceforth, to consider the similar expenditures in
their regular budgeting and programming.

8.18 We recommended that the City Mayor (i) ensure the full or optimum utilization
of the 20% Development Fund during the budget year; (ii) instruct the City
Engineer to conduct through feasibility or preliminary engineering study and
thresh out imminent or possible problems in the implementation of
infrastructure projects that are to be included in the 20% Development Fund
priority list; (iii) require the concerned officers to be prudent in overseeing the
timely implementation of PPAs under their respective responsibility and impose
appropriate sanctions upon the contractors who incur breach of the contract or
agreement; and (iv) instruct the City Accountant to submit immediately to the
Auditor the disbursement vouchers that are due for transmittal.

9.11 We recommended that the management continue to comply with the guidelines
on LDRRMF as prescribed under DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular
No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and instruct the City Accountant to make an
accounting adjustment on the misstatement of affected accounts.

10.14 We reiterated our recommendations that the GAD Focal Point System through
the GAD Focal Person should (a) continue its efforts to improve gender
mainstreaming to achieve full compliance with the provisions of relevant laws
and regulations; (b) employ the Harmonized Gender and Development
Guidelines (HGDG) in the conduct of gender analysis and cost attribution of
City’s PPAs to GAD; and (c) inform the concerned DILG Office, in writing, of
changes introduced in the DILG-endorsed GPB.

11.6 We recommended and the Vice-Chairman of Local School Board agreed to be
mindful of the instructions of the above JC and instruct the City Accountant to
ensure that all disbursements are supported with a declaration, specifically
stating that the expenditure of SEF is net of the budgetary provision for DepEd.

12.14 We reiterated our recommendations that management enhance its compliance
on the publicity and notification of City’s PPAs by: (a) submitting within ten
days the notification to the Auditor on the posting of the appropriate project
signboard and/or public notices in the project site; and (b) requiring the City
Engineering to (i) employ close supervision on the implementation of PPAs, and
maintain a reliable mechanism on maintaining and updating the monthly
reports on PPAs’ accomplishments; and (ii) closely monitor the installation of
signboards/tarpaulins by the contractor/supplier and cause the immediate
rectification had there been incomplete details or erroneous inscription thereon.

13.8 We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant who agreed
to (a) expedite the submission to the Office of the Auditor of the due financial
reports and information including the disbursement vouchers and their
supporting documents; and (b) henceforth, religiously comply with the
reglementary period of submission as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-
006 to avoid the incurrence of delays.

14.8 We recommended that the (a) City Accountant (i) continue complying with the
BIR rules and regulations on the withholding of taxes and (ii) exert efforts to
resolve and settle the unreconciled amount of taxes in the amount of
₱1,171,266.14; and (b) City Accountant and Acting City Treasurer to closely
coordinate and supervise the timely submission of disbursement reports in
order to record the accurate amount of taxes withheld monthly and to remit the
same in full within the prescribed period.

15.17 We recommended that the (a) City Accountant and City Treasurer to continue
to strictly observe the rules and regulations governing the withholding and
remittance of contributions and loan amortizations due to GSIS, PhilHealth and
Pag-IBIG Fund; and (b) City Accountant and Human Resource Management
and Development Officer coordinate with their counterpart from GSIS,
PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG offices to properly address the identified concern such
as the alignment and/or conformity of the City’s collection system with the
Agencies’ computerized billing system to avoid discrepancies between the
City’s collection and Agencies’ billing amount.

18.4 We reiterated that the LCE direct the (a) accountable officials and employees
of the City to exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds
to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances; and (b) City Accountant, in
coordination with City Legal Office, to exhaust all possible means for the
collection/recovery of the disallowed transactions amounting to ₱9,216,704.48
from the persons liable therefrom.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the

(a) Acting City Accountant, City General Service Officer and Supply Officer to maintain respective inventory records and reports such as supplies ledger cards, stock cards and reports of supplies and materials issued respectively; 

(b) Accounting Office to maintain inventory records using the Perpetual Inventory Method for procured supplies and materials; and

(c) Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of supplies and materials at the distribution sites/warehouse at the end of the year to determine the cost of undistributed/unconsumed items that will be restored back to inventory accounts.
observations.managementAction: Lack of Staff and proper storage to organize, archive, compile and update records or details for required ledger accounts.

During the exit conference, the Accounting Office reasoned out that efforts were made to look for pertinent documents relating to the long outstanding reconciling items in the Trust Fund bank accounts, however due to old records and the flood that damaged some records of the City in prior years, the efforts were in vain.  They added that since most of the reconciling items are debit and credit memos from the bank, they also requested from the AGDB the nature of the said transactions, however no reply from AGDB was received.
The City Treasurer also said that she had sent letters to other concerned departments and PNB to inquire of the status of the expropriation proceedings and to determine the possibility of transferring the said deposits to AGDB.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed to

(a) continue its effort to exhaust all possible means for the liquidation and/or recovery of the ₱7,473,005.76 outstanding cash advances; and

(b) endeavor to strengthen the internal control system for cash advances to assure that cash advances are liquidated or reported on as soon as the purpose of the same is served, in compliance with Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular 97-002, in order to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and the Local Chief Executive agreed to

(a) direct the Acting City Accountant to initiate thorough review and trace the original documents and recording of non-moving old Due to National Government Agencies (NGAs) account balances in order to determine the details such as the name of the source agency for eventual reconciliation and to effect the necessary adjustments/corrections, if any;

(b) remind the Acting City Accountant and the City Treasurer to cause the immediate return of the unutilized and/ or non- moving funds to the respective source agencies in observance of Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated  December 13, 1994; and

(c) require the Heads of the Offices concerned to strictly observe the time frame of scheduled implementation of the projects to ensure efficient delivery of public services to the intended beneficiaries and to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future.
observations.managementAction: The original documents which should serve as bases for the return of the balances to the concerned NGAs could not be retrieved due to the collective years of incomplete turnover of files from the previous official.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to continue the inclusion and prioritization of high-impact developmental projects in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) of the City Government when COVID-19 emergency restrictions eases. We further recommend that the LCE direct the

(a) Bids and Awards Committee  to

(i) furnish the Audit Team copies of screenshot of postings in the GPPB online portal for various emergency procurement and Business/ Mayor’s Permit for the  hotels that catered to COVID patients and front liners;

(ii) submit an explanation on the payment of utilities expenses to hotel providers for accommodations of COVID patients and front liners which is not included in the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA); and

(b) City Engineer to determine the viability of the continuation of the projects that were not implemented during the COVID-19 pandemic, henceforth to closely monitor/supervise its implementation to ensure completion within the contract time for the immediate utilization of the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the

(a) Acting City Accountant to observe the prescribe period in the submission of reports and supporting documents as stated in Section 7.2.1 Item (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006;

(b) Bids and Awards Committee to ensure submission of pertinent procurement documents and requirements of suppliers for timely validation by the Audit Team; and

(c) Local Finance Committee to post the required report to City’s website to enhance transparency of transactions as stated in Section 4.0 of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125.

observations.managementAction: The CSWDO in her reply with the issued AOM, explained and submitted schedule that 338 out of 422 names of the SAP beneficiaries were misspelled in the SAC masterlist; 14 returned their SAP subsidy and were not encoded in the masterlist; 5 beneficiaries have errors in encoding; 7 beneficiaries were reported as unpaid hence not encoded in the masterlist; 38 SAC forms were not encoded; and 20 are either replacement or representatives authorized by the beneficiaries.  The updated masterlist was already prepared by the CSWDO to correct the noted errors in encoding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed that the (a) City Accountant to strictly adhere to the fundamental principles in the disbursement of funds as prescribed under Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and submit the above-mentioned lacking documents of concerned employees such as the Accomplishment Reports and Daily Timer Records in coordination with City HRMO and department heads; (b) officials preparing the payroll and Accounting Office to ensure that claims are properly and correctly computed prior to disbursements to avoid overpayments; and (c) concerned employees to refund the excess payment of COVID-19 Hazard Pay.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed that the (a) City Planning and Development Officer (CPDO), City Engineer and other concerned departments to (i) strictly monitor the status of implementation of their Programs/ Projects/ Activities (PPAs) to ensure its completion within the contract period; (ii) submit to the Audit Team the corrected consolidated Report on Government PPAs with complete details including the Project Status report as required in Section 3.2 and Annex A of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 and copies of Suspension Orders; and (b) City Engineer to compute for liquidated damages, if any, in coordination with the Accounting Unit, of every projects with unjustified delay in the completion and charge the amount to the respective claims of the contractors concerned accordingly.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the Bids and Awards Committee and City Human Resource Management Office to submit copies of Contracts and Purchase Orders together with its supporting documents within five (5) days from its execution and/or issuance in accordance with COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 for the timely conduct by the Audit Team of contract review, inspection and other necessary audit procedures.

We recommended and the GAD Focal Point System through the GAD Focal Person agreed to (a) continue to (i) develop and implement more GAD related programs/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality and improve the welfare and protection of rights of women; and (ii) strictly adhere to PCW-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-01 in the implementation of planned projects and maximum utilization of appropriated funds for the purpose; and (b) attach the result of gender analysis through the use of Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) in the attribution of costs of regular program/project in the submission of GAD budget and accomplishment report.

We recommended that the Management continue complying with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding of taxes on compensation and value added taxes, percentage taxes and expanded taxes from government money payments to assure that the government was not deprived of taxes that can be used in the implementation of various programs and projects.  We also recommended that the Accounting and Treasury Offices to strictly observe the rules and regulations governing the intact and full remittance of taxes withheld.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the

(a) Accounting and Treasury Offices to continue to strictly observe the rules and regulations governing the withholding and remittance of contributions and loan amortizations due to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG Fund;

(b) Accounting and Human Resource Management and Development Offices coordinate with their counterpart from GSIS and PhilHealth offices to properly address the identified concern such as the alignment and/or conformity of the City’s collection system with the new computerized billing system of the said agencies to avoid discrepancies between the City’s collection and agencies’ billing amount; and 

(c) Accounting Office to analyze and identify the causes of the higher unremitted year-end balances of due to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-Ibig which can be attributed to the previous year’s forwarded balances; henceforth make the necessary adjustments thereof, if warranted.

We recommended and the LCE concurred to direct the (a) accountable officials and employees of the City to exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances; and (b) Acting City Accountant, in coordination with City Legal Office, to exhaust all possible means for the collections/recovery of the disallowed transactions amounting to ₱9,216,704.48 from the persons liable therefrom.

We reiterated our recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed to-
 
instruct the City Accountant to initiate thorough review and trace the original documents and recordings of non-moving old account balances and effect the necessary adjustments/corrections, if any, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts in the books of the agency; 

remind the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to cause the immediate return  of the unutilized and/ or non- moving funds to the respective source  Agencies (NGAs) in observance of Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated  December 13, 1994; and

require the Heads of the Offices concerned to strictly observe the time frame of scheduled implementation of the projects to ensure efficient delivery of public services to the intended beneficiaries and to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed that the (a) City Treasurer to (i) strictly monitor the implementation of the action plan on conducting public auction and issuance of warranty of levy as remedies for the collection of delinquent RPT as provided in Section 254 of R.A. No. 7160; and (ii) on a timely basis, prepare an updated Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due and submit the same to the Accounting Office as the basis in setting-up of RPT/SET receivables in the books of accounts at the beginning of the year; (b) Acting City Accountant and City Treasurer to reconcile their records of RPT/SET receivables for reliable account balances in the financial statements; (c) Acting City Accountant use the Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due prepared by the CTO as the basis in recording the receivables at the beginning of the year; and (d) City Assessor to coordinate and on a timely basis, provide the  CTO with the needed assessment reports to facilitate the preparation of Certified List of Taxpayers by the latter within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) Inventory Committee, in coordination with the CGSO Head and all the department heads and offices, to exert extra effort to complete the physical inventory of all PPE of the City Government and reconcile the report with the accounting records; (b) CGSO Head to (i) strictly comply with the required maintenance of complete and updated property records; (ii) observe the annual submission of the physical inventory reports on PPE to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 266 of GAM for LGUs, Volume I for timely review and validation thereof; and (iii) immediately notify the Audit Team and submit the required documents  for Request for Relief of Accountability within the deadline, whenever a loss of property occurs; and (c) Acting City Accountant to (i) coordinate with the CGSO and Inventory Committee for the reconciliation of respective records and effect the necessary corrections/adjustments, if warranted, in the books of accounts; and (ii) transfer the completed projects from the CIP account to appropriate asset accounts.

We recommended and the LCE concurred that the (a) Acting City Accountant, CGSO Head and Supply Officer to maintain their respective inventory records and reports such as SLCs, SCs and RSMI, respectively; (b) Acting City Accountant to maintain inventory records using the Perpetual Inventory Method for procured supplies and materials; and (c) Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of supplies and materials at the distribution sites/warehouse at the end of the year to determine the cost of undistributed/unconsumed items that will be restored back to inventory accounts.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) Acting City Accountant to exert more effort in determining the nature and details of the long-outstanding bank reconciling items amounting to ₱106,393.90 to facilitate the immediate adjustments of the same in the book of accounts; (b) City Treasurer, in coordination with the concerned departments who are in charge of expropriation proceedings, to follow-up their status with the concerned trial courts to determine whether the claims are still valid since the deposits for the purpose is non-moving for more than ten years and to further determine the possibility of transferring the said deposits to AGDBs.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Acting City Accountant (a) in coordination with City Legal Office, continue their effort to exhaust all possible means for the liquidation and/or recovery of the ₱7,473,005.76 outstanding cash advances; and (b) endeavor to strengthen the internal control system for CAs to assure that these are liquidated or reported on as soon as the purpose of the same are served to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) through the Acting City Accountant, file a request for authority to write-off dormant and uncollectible receivable accounts to the COA with the supporting documents required under Item 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005; (b) instruct the Acting City Accountant and CSWD Officer to reconcile their records in order to effect the necessary adjustment/correction, if any; and (c) direct the CSWD Officer to (i) revisit the Kasunduan governing the implementation of SEA program and ensure that the provisions stipulated therein are strictly observed to protect the interest of the government; and (ii) through the City Legal Officer, revise the format of the Kasunduan to include the loan amount, payment terms, payment method, a penal provision and further information on the borrower and the co-maker.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and the LCE concurred to (a) direct the Acting City Accountant to initiate thorough review and trace the original documents and recording of non-moving old Due to National Government Agencies (NGAs) account balances in order to determine the details such as the name of the source agency for eventual reconciliation and to effect the necessary adjustments/corrections, if any; (b) remind the Acting City Accountant and the City Treasurer to cause the immediate return of the unutilized and/or non- moving funds to the respective source agencies in observance of Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013; and (c) require the Heads of the Offices concerned to strictly observe the time frame of scheduled implementation of the projects to ensure efficient delivery of public services to the intended beneficiaries and to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct (a) City Budget Officer to provide satisfactory explanation/justification on the purchase of laboratory and other medical supplies which was charged to budget for various commodities for relief operations as approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod (SP) under the Supplemental Budget (SB) No. 04, series of 2020; (b) City Health Officer to determine the necessity of purchasing additional ventilators as approved by the SP under the SB No. 04, series of 2020, otherwise consider the realignment/reprogram of the unimplemented project; (c)  BAC to (i) ensure that the POs executed and conformed by the suppliers are compliant with GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 for the purchase of goods; and (ii) submit proof of posting of updated APP and NOA in GPPB online portal on the noted procurement; (d) Acting City Accountant to (i) attach brochures/photographs of supplies/materials purchased as required by Section 9.1.3.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (ii) consistently record the laboratory and medical supplies purchased as Inventory following the Perpetual Inventory Method; and (e) City Social Welfare and Development Officer to (i) prepare schedule of inventory of donations in the required format and submit within ten working days after the end of each quarter to the NDRRMC, through the OCD, copy furnished the Audit Team as required in Section II(a) and (b) of COA Circular No. 2020-009; (ii) utilize the unused cash donation amounting to ₱30,000.00 received from private donor for COVID-19 related PPAs; (iii) submit distributions list/acknowledgment receipts on the rice given to various groups/institution/sector for random validation of the Audit Team; (iv) submit report/certification on the consumption of dressed chicken in the community kitchen with details such as the dates consumed and list of volunteers/frontliners given with meals; and (v) submit distribution lists/list of recipients of food packs given to other beneficiaries aside from households.

We recommended and the LCE is amenable that the (a) Chairman, BAC to (i) ensure that the necessary procurement information are timely posted in GPPB portal and PhilGEPS; (ii) utilize the City’s website in the posting of procurement opportunities and information; and (iii) include warranty clause in the PO and require all suppliers to submit OSS in accordance with Guidelines for Emergency Procurement under Bayanihan to Heal as One Act; and (b) Acting City Accountant to observe the proper recognition in the books of the procured equipment and inventory in accordance with Section 175, Chapter 6 and Section 208, Chapter 7, GAM for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended and the LCE concurred to continue the inclusion and prioritization of high-impact developmental projects in the AIP of the City Government when COVID-19 emergency restrictions eases. We further recommended that the LCE direct the (a) BAC to furnish the Audit Team copies of screenshot of postings in the GPPB online portal for various emergency procurement and Business/ Mayor’s Permit for the  hotels that catered to COVID-19 patients and front liners; (b) City Health Officer submit an explanation on the payment of utilities expenses to hotel providers for accommodations of COVID-19 patients which is not included in the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA); and (c) City Engineer to (i) determine the viability of the continuation of the projects that were not implemented during the COVID-19 pandemic; and (ii) henceforth, to closely monitor/supervise its implementation to ensure completion within the contract time for the immediate utilization of the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) Acting City Accountant to observe the prescribed period in the submission of reports and supporting documents as stated in Item(a) of Section 7.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006; (b) BAC to (i) ensure submission of pertinent procurement documents and requirements of suppliers for timely validation of the Audit Team; and (c) LFC to post the required report to City’s website to enhance transparency of transactions as stated in Section 4.0 of DBM LBC No. 125.

We recommended that the LCE direct the CSWD Officer to (a) provide satisfactory explanation on the (i) non-inclusion in the SAC master list of some paid beneficiaries of SAP; and (ii) non-issuance of ORs to all beneficiaries who returned the received SAP; (b) provide copies of the particular ARs to the Audit Team; and (c) submit copy of report/information/certification to the Audit Team that the (i) 18 beneficiaries who are members of 4Ps as listed in the SAC master list were evaluated to ensure that no duplication of assistance was given; and (ii) criteria used in the evaluation of their qualifications of the ten respondents who did not confirm receipt of the SAP.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Acting City Accountant strictly adhere to the fundamental principles in the disbursement of funds as prescribed under Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and submit the above-mentioned lacking documents of concerned employees such as the Accomplishment Reports and Daily Timer Records, in coordination with City HRMO and department heads.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) City Planning and Development Officer (CPDO), City Engineer and other concerned departments to (i) strictly monitor the status of implementation of their PPAs to ensure their completion within the contracted period; and (ii) submit to the Audit Team the corrected consolidated Report on Government PPAs with complete details including the Project Status report as required in Section 3.2 and Annex A of COA Circular No. 2013-004 and copies of Suspension Orders; and (b) City Engineer to compute for liquidated damages, if any, in coordination with the Accounting Unit, for all projects with unjustified delay in the completion and deduct the amount to the respective claims of the contractors concerned accordingly.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the CGSO Head and City Human Resource Management Officer to submit copies of Contracts and POs together with their supporting documents within five (5) days from their execution and/or issuance in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-001 for the timely conduct by the Audit Team of contract review, inspection and other necessary audit procedures.

We recommended and the GAD Focal Point System, through the GAD Focal Person, agreed to (a) continue to (i) develop and implement more GAD related programs/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality and improve the welfare and protection of rights of women; and (ii) strictly adhere to DBM-NEDA-NCRFW JC No. 2004-01 in the implementation of planned projects and maximum utilization of appropriated funds for the purpose; and (b) attach the result of gender analysis through the use of HGDG in the attribution of costs of regular programs/projects in the submission of GAD budget and AR.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) Acting City Accountant continue complying with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding of taxes on compensation and value added taxes, percentage taxes and expanded taxes from government money payments; and (b) Acting City Accountant and City Treasurer to strictly observe the rules and regulations governing the intact and full remittance of taxes withheld.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) Acting City Accountant and City Treasurer to continue to strictly observe the rules and regulations governing the withholding and remittance of contributions and loan amortizations due to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG Fund; (b) Acting City Accountant and Human Resource Management and Development Officer coordinate with their counterpart from GSIS and PhilHealth offices to properly address the identified concern such as the alignment and/or conformity of the City’s collection system with the new computerized billing system of the said agencies to avoid discrepancies between the City’s collection and agencies’ billing amount; and (c) Acting City Accountant to (i) analyze and identify the causes of the higher unremitted year-end balances of due to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG which can be attributed to the previous year’s forwarded balances; and (ii) henceforth, make the necessary adjustments thereof, if warranted.

We recommended and the LCE concurred to direct the (a) accountable officials and employees of the City to exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances; and (b) Acting City Accountant, in coordination with City Legal Office, to exhaust all possible means for the collections/recovery of the disallowed transactions amounting to ₱9,216,704.48 from the persons liable therefrom.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct:

The City Treasurer:

introduce collection strategy that would significantly help increase the RPT/SEF tax collection of the City; 




coordinate with the City Accounting Office for the reconciliation of their respective records and to effect the necessary adjustments/corrections; 



timely prepare an updated Certified List of Taxpayers, bearing the names, particulars of the property, location, amount due and collectible for the applicable year and other pertinent information; and submit the same to the accounting unit as the basis in setting up of RPT/SEF tax receivable in the books of accounts of the City at the beginning of the year; 

ensure that the list of taxpayers bear vital information, such as the exact addresses, to facilitate, if necessary, the sending of notices and other follow up communications relative to tax campaign activities and other collection strategy that may be  implemented by Management; and

use available remedies, administrative and/ or judicial action, for the collection of taxes as provided in Section 256 of the Local Government Code of 1991.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
City Treasurer to (i) prepare an updated Certified List of Taxpayers, bearing the names, amount due and collectible during the year, and submit the same to the accounting unit as the basis in setting up of RPT/SET receivable in the succeeding year; (ii) ensure that the list of taxpayers bear vital information, such as the addresses, to facilitate, if necessary, the sending of notices and other follow-up communications relative to tax campaign activities and other collection strategy that may be  implemented by Management; and (iii) introduce collection strategy that would significantly help increase the tax collection of the City.
City Accountant to (i) observe the provisions of Section 20 of the New Government Accounting (NGAS) Manual; and (ii) use the Certified List of Taxpayers and tax due prepared by the Office of the City Treasurer as the basis in recording the RPT/SET receivables at the beginning of the year.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Accountant, City Engineer and the City Treasurer coordinate with each other to trace the records/documents of the completed projects which were still included in the Construction in Progress account to facilitate the necessary recording under the appropriate asset accounts and to effect the necessary adjustments for fair presentation of balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Head of the General Services Office to (a) cause the preparation of the updated Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property together with the corresponding assessment, evaluation and appraisal reports; (b) facilitate the submission of request for disposal of said PPEs to the Office of the Auditor for inspection, evaluation and review by the COA Technical Services Office; (c) ensure that after receipt of favorable COA recommendation on the request,  undertake immediate disposal of the subject properties to prevent further deterioration, decline of value and in order to generate additional funds from sale thereof; and (d) prepare and submit the regular schedule of disposal of assets once the same became unserviceable and beyond economical repairs to avoid the accumulation of unserviceable PPEs in the storage facility and to prevent the recurrence of similar lapses.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the (a) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to thoroughly double check the completeness of the required documents submitted by the bidders; (b) Office of the City Accountant to be more diligent in ensuring the completeness of the required supporting documents prior to its indorsement to the Office of the LCE for final approval; (c) City Engineering Office conduct constant site inspection and strict monitoring on the status of implementation to prevent unnecessary delay in the completion of projects; and (d) the City Engineer and the 
City Accountant to observe the imposition of liquidated damages for unjustified delay in completion of project, if warranted, to protect the interest of the City Government and to compel the contractor to complete the projects at the earliest possible time.

We recommended and management agreed that:
The Local Chief Executive (i) reiterate his directive to the City Administrator, City Accountant and City Legal Office for the immediate issuance of the necessary follow-up Demand Letters to the persons responsible/liable for the liquidation of the ₱ 18,080,532.96 outstanding cash advances; (ii) require City Accountant to (1) ensure timely submission of the liquidation reports and/or settlement of the same to the Office of the COA Auditor for review; and (2) endeavor to strengthen the internal control system for cash advances, or for all other assets of the City for that matter, which would assure that cash advances are liquidated or reported on as soon as the purpose of the same is served, in compliance with Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular 97-002, to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses.
If warranted, file charges against the persons liable in the proper Court for failure to settle their respective accountabilities despite repeated demand.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Treasurer take the necessary action to ensure replacement or collection of the dishonored checks received from the taxpayer/payee. Further, the City Accountant effect the adjusting entries in the books of the dishonored checks received as payment to the city government which were subsequently returned by the bank due to insufficient funds and/or closed account. Henceforth, we also recommended that both Offices consistently observe diligence in the preparation of quarterly reconciliation statement to immediately detect any error/discrepancy for timely adjustment and accordingly, to establish an accurate and reliable records and reports on Cash in Treasury balance.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Treasurer and City Accountant strictly observe the internal control processes thru the participation of the proper authorities mentioned above in approving changes and/ or effecting entries in the automated system.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Accountant (a) follow up the prior communications/coordination with the concerned officials of the servicing banks for the timely delivery and/ or submission of the Bank Statements together with scanned copy of negotiated checks, credit and debit memos and other supporting documents; and (b) ensure the prompt preparation and submission of the BRS to the Office of the Auditor within the required period for timely review and verification of the correctness of the reported cash in bank balances every end of the reporting month.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to (i) ensure the correctness of the contents of Notices prior to posting at PhilGEPS website; and (ii) thoroughly double check the completeness and accuracy of the technical and financial proposals submitted by the bidders; (b) City Engineering Office to (i) be more diligent in the preparation of documents by ensuring all necessary data and bases of computation of ABC are considered in order to arrive at the amount most advantageous to the government; (ii) specify in the Bill of Quantities (BOQ) the  items of works to be performed and the respective quantities of materials needed per scope of work; (iii) make detailed estimates of every costs in the Program of Works (POW); and (iv) conduct constant site inspection and strict monitoring on the status of implementation to prevent unnecessary delay in the completion of projects; and (iii) the City Engineer and the City Accountant to observe the imposition of corresponding liquidated damages, if warranted, to protect the interest of the City Government and to compel the contractor to complete the projects within the contract period.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to:
ensure the timely posting of Notices at PhilGEPS website; and
thoroughly double check the completeness and accuracy of the technical and financial proposals by ensuring that all necessary data and bases of computation are considered in the bids submitted by the bidders, and;
Office of the City Accountant to be more diligent in ensuring the completeness of the required supporting documents prior to its indorsement to the Office of the LCE for final approval.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (a) instruct the BAC Chairperson to submit written explanation on the occurrence of repeated errors and/or misrepresentation in the publication of Invitation to Bid (ITBs); (b) adopt internal control systems/measures that will ensure the correctness of the invitation to bid posted by the BAC to prevent the incurrence of similar lapses; (c) if possible, assign additional support staff in the BAC Secretariat unit that will help facilitate timely review of entries prior to posting at the website; (d) conduct verification/investigation to determine the real reasons and the persons primarily accountable behind the noted errors in publication, if needed; and (e) consider the upgrading of internet services to cope up with the  need in facilitating the required publication of procurement activities of the agency, if necessary.

We recommended and management agreed that Local Chief Executive (i) require the City General Services Officer (GSO) to consistently observe submission of approved contracts/ purchase orders together with the supporting documents within five working days upon approval as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to facilitate timely review and evaluation; and (ii) direct compliance with COA Circular 95-006 dated  May 18, 1995 in the submission of  Inspection and Acceptance Reports (IAR) to prevent the recurrence of similar lapses and to facilitate the conduct of  inspection/validation of delivery in the future and to timely communicate to management the deficiencies, if any, for corrective action.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to (a) strictly adhere to the provision of COA Circular No. 2009-006 on the submission of reports, accounts and transaction documents for post-audit; and (b) coordinate and adopt more efficient procedures that will ensure timely and regular submission of the required documents and reports to the Office of the COA Auditor for review and evaluation and in order to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the Chairman and Members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to observe prudence in the performance of their respective duties and functions,  particularly in (a) determining the reasonable prices to be used in setting up of Budget for the Contract which should be the most advantageous to the government; (b) conducting evaluation by considering all factors, such as the specification, brand, prices and others that may contribute in rendering decision favorable to the agency; and (c) preventing the recurrence of similar lapses to avoid wastage of government resources.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (i) direct the City Planning and Development Officer to monitor the effective and efficient implementation of the projects under the 20% Development Fund so that necessary interventions by the implementing offices may be made for their timely completion; (ii) ensure that development projects for inclusion in the 20% Development Fund have been thoroughly studied and prioritized; and (iii) continue to invest in high–impact or developmental projects for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan.

We recommended and management agreed that the CSWMB, CGSO and City Accountant (a) in coordination with other offices involve, submit the complete documentary deficiencies; and (b) ensure that the required documents for each type of transaction are submitted and/or attached to the DVs before processing and payment of claim.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
instruct the City Treasurer to regularly conduct examination on the books of accounts of business establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales/receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid and to collect the correct taxes as provided under Section 171 of the Local Revenue Code; and
assign personnel at the CTO who are equipped with the necessary knowledge and training in the examination of books of accounts of the business establishments to enable said Office in carrying on with their mandate efficiently.

We recommended and management agreed to (a) direct the CSWDO and the City Accountant to (i) strictly adhere to the fundamental principles in the disbursement of funds prescribed under Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445; (ii) observe full compliance with the requirements consistent with the guidelines of the DSWD in the implementation Cash for Work for Climate Change  Adaption and Mitigation Program; (iii) facilitate the immediate submission of validated daily accomplishment reports together with the certified pictures taken during the actual implementation of the program; and (b) direct the Disbursing Officer concerned to explain in writing the deficiencies noted on the questionable signatures of claimants appearing in the daily time records and IDs.
We likewise recommended that payments to cash for work laborers, and other workers like job orders, casuals, etc. should be made by the designated Disbursing Officer after the presentation of Identification Card to ensure fulfillment of obligation to right persons and prevent fictitious transactions.

We recommended and management agreed that (a) the LCPC/CCWPC submit the reports and documents  required under DILG MC 	No. 2008-126 and DILG MC No. 2012-120, respectively; (b) the CSWD and City Budget Officer adhere with the provisions of RA 9344 on the required allocation of the amount equivalent to 1% of IRA for the purpose; and (c) conduct timely planning and implementation of the programs, projects and activities for the child protection and development to efficiently deliver the services to the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to:
continuously  observe the rules and regulations governing the withholding of taxes, premium contributions, etc. and accordingly improve remittances of the same to the respective government agencies concerned, the BIR, GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG;
regularly coordinate to facilitate processing of documents necessary to support the completeness of remittance, recording of transactions and timely transmission of disbursement vouchers to the respective Offices concerned; and
exert extra effort in identifying the nature of the previous years’ balances due to GSIS and PhilHealth and make the necessary adjustment thereof to facilitate the immediate remittance of the same to the concerned agencies.

We recommended and management agreed to exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances. We further recommended that management cause the total settlement of the PEI overpayment.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that the: 

Local Chief Executive (i) direct the concerned City officials and traffic management personnel under the PNP’s supervision, to fully enforce the existing City ordinance in prohibiting/banning the plying of tricycles along the national highway of the City, particularly on inner lanes, to prevent any hazardous incidence thru road accidents, which may pose imminent danger both to pedestrians and motorists’ lives and properties and to ease the traffic congestion which resulted from the plying of many tricycles in the national highways; 

(ii) take into consideration the installation of an adequate and efficient CCTVs along the national highways, including the nearby barangays of the City as an effective monitoring tool to assess/evaluate traffic management and capture data on any occurrence of criminal acts and in providing immediate assistance to the public in case of road accidents/criminalities; and 

(iii) direct the City Human Resource Officer and the City General Service Officer to consider the necessity of hiring additional traffic personnel to man the critical or traffic/accident-prone areas and provide the necessary communication equipment and facilities to monitor incidents that require immediate action/rescue by the City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office; and

Members of the SP revisit their existing ordinances to make necessary amendments or enact appropriate ordinance on the regulation for a more effective operation of tricycles within the City by taking into consideration the total prohibition/banning of tricycles to operate/ply their routes on the City national highways, provided that there are identified alternative routes, pursuant to the directives and guidelines of the DILG and DOTC for public safety and order in both pedestrians and motorists.
observations.managementAction: CY 2016 Audit Observation No. 12 |

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management require the (a) CGSO to strictly comply with the required maintenance of complete and updated PPELC and PC, respectively for each class of property and to record the acquisition, adjustments and disposal thereof; (b) Inventory Committee exert extra effort to complete the physical inventory of all property, plant, and equipment of the City Government and reconcile the report with the accounting records; and (c) GSO observe the consistent submission of the physical inventory reports on PPEs to the Office of the Auditor for review and validation not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to existing rules and regulations;

Moreover, the City Accountant, City Assessor and Heads of the General Services Office (GSO) and City Planning and Development Office (CPDO) (a) coordinate with each other and reconcile their records to ascertain the correctness of the Land account balance; (b) facilitate the registration in the name of City Government the parcel of lots acquired which were not yet covered by TCT to preclude third party claims over said properties; and (c) the Accounting Office to record in the books of accounts all unrecorded parcels of land acquired by the City and maintain Real Property Ledger Card for each and every parcel of land/PPEs owned by the City.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Accountant, City Engineer and the City Treasurer coordinate with each other to trace the records/documents of the completed projects which were still included in the Construction in Progress account to facilitate the necessary recording under the appropriate asset accounts and to effect the necessary adjustments for fair presentation of balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting Unit to immediately cause reconciliation of records and accordingly effect adjustments, if any, in the book of accounts of the City. She was also instructed to implement the recommendations offered by the Audit Team to address the deficiencies discussed in the aforesaid meeting.
The City Hospital Administrator, in his letter to this Office, manifested that they have initiated efforts to speed up the preparation and filing of claims for the Philhealth benefits, as well as the preparation of the abstract for the distribution of the collected amount to the concerned persons or offices. He assured that the Office of the Hospital Administrator is cognizant of the good governance advocacy of the City Government. Compliance with the recommendation on the immediate submission of updated list of medical doctors with pending claims on professional fees was also mentioned in the said communication.
We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting Unit to immediately cause reconciliation of records and immediately effect the necessary adjustment in order to properly reflect the correct account balances of the affected Due to PhilHealth and Other Payable accounts in the financial statements of the City. Likewise, prepare and submit to PhilHealth the required liquidation report on the utilization of PCF in accordance with PhilHealth Circular No. 40 s. 2000.
Further, require the City Hospital officials concerned to cause the timely submission of claims of medical doctors together with the complete supporting documents to facilitate its processing and early release of the same. Likewise, submit to the accounting unit the updated list of medical doctors with pending claims on professional fees filed with PhilHealth in order to properly monitor the status of their claim.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Head of the General Services Office to (a) cause the preparation of the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property together with the corresponding assessment, evaluation and appraisal reports; (b) facilitate the submission of request for disposal of said PPEs to the Office of the Auditor for inspection, evaluation and review by the COA Technical Services Office; (c) undertake immediate disposal of the subject properties to prevent further deterioration, decline of value and in order to generate additional funds from sale thereof after receipt of favorable COA recommendation on the submitted request; and (d)  require the GSO to prepare and submit the regular schedule of disposal of assets once the same became unserviceable and beyond economical repairs to avoid the accumulation of unserviceable PPEs in the storage facility and to prevent the recurrence of similar lapses.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Accountant and City Treasurer consistently observe diligence in the preparation of quarterly reconciliation statement to immediately detect any error/discrepancy for timely adjustment and accordingly, to establish an accurate and reliable records and reports on Cash in Treasury balance.

We recommended and management agreed that the amount of bond of the liquidating officers be increased based on their respective cash accountabilities and applicable rates provided in the Revised Schedule of Premium Rates per Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 on fidelity bond.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Accountant (a) coordinate with the concerned officials of the servicing banks for the timely delivery and/ or submission of the Bank Statements with supporting documents; and (b) ensure the prompt preparation and submission of the BRS to the Office of the Auditor within the required period for timely review and verification to ascertain the correctness of the reported cash in bank balances every end of the reporting month.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct:

the City Administrator and City Accountant to coordinate with the City Legal Office for the immediately issuance of the necessary follow up Demand Letters to the persons responsible/liable for the liquidation of the ₱ 16,821,660.35 outstanding cash advances.

the City Accountant:
ensure timely submission of the liquidation reports and/or settlement of the same to the Office of the COA Auditor for review. The required documents must be attached to the liquidation reports, such as the official receipts, certificate of seminar/trainings completed, etc. in accordance with COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and
avoid the recurrence of similar lapses, endeavor to strengthen the internal control system for cash advances, or for all other assets of the City for that matter, which would assure that cash advances are liquidated or reported on as soon as the purpose of the same is served, in compliance with Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular 97-002.
the filing of charges against the persons liable in the proper Court for failure to settle their respective accountabilities upon demand, if warranted.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Mayor enjoin:

the concerned department heads’ (City Engineer, General Services Officer and the City Accountant) compliance with the requirements of COA Circular 2015-008;

the City Accountant to coordinate with the City Engineer and the General Services Officer in the preparation of the Local Road Network Ledger Card (LRNLC) and include in the disclosure the total road networks in the Notes to the Financial Statements; and

the City General Services Officer to (i) coordinate with the City Engineering Office in the conduct of inventory of local road network yearly; (ii)  prepare Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System, Report on Local Road Network, and (iii) to maintain the Road Map and Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to (i) strictly monitor the timely implementation of the approved programmed plans and activities identified in the LDRRM Plan for the maximum utilization of the LDRRM Fund in order to achieve the target accomplishment for the year; (ii) ensure that all implemented programs/projects and activities charged to LDRRMF were in line with the LDRRM Plan; and (iii) formulate programs and activities strictly in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 for proper allocation and utilization of LDRRMF.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Government,  through the City Development Council (i) ensure that the mandatory 20% Development Fund appropriated out of the Internal Revenue Allotment be fully utilized for the implementation of the prioritized identified programs, projects and activities to help achieve the desirable outcomes on socio-economic development and environmental management pursuant to the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1; and (ii) come up with a good planning in identifying priority programs and projects and strict observance of scheduled implementation for the immediate benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended and management agreed that the GAD Focal Person continue to (i) develop and implement more GAD related programs/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality and improve welfare of women that will protect their rights; (ii) exert more diligence in identifying priority programs/projects during the planning and budget preparation to avoid sacrificing the implementation of priority programs; and (iii) strictly adhere to PCW-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-01 in the implementation of planned projects.

We recommended and management agreed that (a) the LCPC/CCWPC submit the reports and documents  required under DILG MC No. 2008-126 and DILG MC No. 2012-120, respectively; (b) the CSWD and City Budget Officer to adhere with the provisions of RA 9344, on the required allocation of the amount equivalent to 1% of IRA for the purpose; and (c) conduct timely planning and implementation of the programs, projects and activities for the child protection and development to efficiently deliver the services to the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Solid Waste Management Board (CSWMB) and the Environmental Protection Division (EPD) (i) exert effort to expedite the establishment and completion of the Integrated Solid Waste Management Facility; (ii) involve closely the Barangay officials in the strict implementation of the existing environmental laws and intensify the information campaign on the “no segregation, no collection policy and the “no-littering policy” among the residents; and (iii) exercise strong political will to implement the environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment as mandated under Republic Act  No. 9003.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the -
(a) BAC to immediately submit copy of the lacking documents, such as the (i) Instructions to Bidders, (ii) Bid Data Sheet, (iii) General and Special Conditions of Contract, and (iv) Technical Specifications and to observe diligence in the performance of their duties and responsibilities to prevent the recurrence of similar lapses; and
(b) City Accountant to strictly adhere to the fundamental principles in the disbursement of funds prescribed under Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 and coordinate with the BAC for the early compliance with the required submission of complete supporting documents on the audited disbursements totaling ₱14,310,964.19.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive, thru the Finance Committee, consider the preparation of proposal, for approval of the Sangguniang Panglunsod (SP), similar to the best practices being adopted by the neighboring LGUs on revenue generating measure, particularly the charging of fee on land use reclassification, to further strengthen the financial capability of the city government.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (a) direct the Head of the City Economic Enterprise and Public Utilities and Management Office (CEEPUMO) (i) to coordinate with the City Legal Office for the issuance of the necessary follow up/ demand letters to the accountable delinquent stall holders/occupants for the settlement of delinquent accounts; (ii) initiate legal action, if warranted, to collect the long overdue arrearages; and (iii) strictly enforce the provisions of the contract/agreement in order to prevent the recurrence of similar problem in the future; (b) require the CEEPUMO to facilitate the submission of an updated master list of stallholders and reconcile the same with the actual occupants and numbers of market stalls appearing in the structure layout plan of the two markets to clearly establish that all units are properly accounted, recorded and included in the monthly report of market collections and prevent the re-leasing of the stalls.
We also recommended that management revisit the rental rates on market stalls/space and consider the possible adjustments to an equitable  level and  comparable with the prevailing current rates being adopted in the locality.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the LCE remind the GSO to (a) strictly observe the submission of Notice of Delivery, together with copy of Delivery Receipt and duly accomplished/signed Inspection and Acceptance Report, to the Office of Auditor within 24 hours upon receipt of items delivered by the supplier pursuant to COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995; and (b) adopt improvement measures, such as systematic pre-delivery repacking of commodities by the supplier to ensure that procured commodities will be timely and completely distributed to the intended beneficiaries in order to effectively serve its purpose.
We also recommended that the CSWD Officer a) strictly observe the provisions stated in the specific implementing agreement on Supplemental Feeding Program; b) explain in writing the discrepancies noted on the duplication and/or simultaneous appearance of names of several beneficiaries in the weighing monitoring report under two different barangays for the same period; c) conduct post-evaluation and validation and submit the corresponding reports; and c) coordinate with the BAC, GSO and Accounting Office for the immediate submission of the lacking documents to fully comply with the documentary requirements.

In view of the foregoing deficiencies, we recommended and management agreed that the CSWDO and the City Accountant (a) cause the immediate submission of the required supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for review and evaluation; and (b) strictly adhere to the fundamental principles in the disbursement of funds prescribed under Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 particularly on the submission of complete documentation for every claim against government funds.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Mayor instruct the (a) City Accountant to (i) cause the immediate submission of the required supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for review and evaluation; and (ii) strictly adhere to the fundamental principles in the disbursement of funds prescribed under Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 particularly on the submission of complete documents for every claim against government funds to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses; (b) City General Services Officer (CGSO) to (i) strictly observe the regulation on submission of Notice of Delivery, together with copy of Delivery Receipt and duly accomplished/signed Inspection and Acceptance Report, to the Office of the COA Auditor within 24 hours upon receipt of items delivered to the City storeroom/warehouse; and (ii) ensure that various commodities are properly distributed only to identified beneficiaries/recipients.

We recommended and Management agreed to exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances. We further recommended for the total settlement of PEI overpayment.
